category: Frameworks, mobile, games, hardware, and creative ecosystems

surface.name: Official Arduino IDE sketch upload path
surface.selection_basis: The Arduino documentation home prominently routes new developers to `Set Up Your Arduino Board`, and the official Help Center publishes a platform-level guide titled `Upload a sketch in Arduino IDE`. That guide accounts for board-package and port variation, offers the built-in Blink example, and ends with the uploaded sketch starting on the board. Arduino separately recommends the current IDE 2 release.

alternatives_considered.name: Arduino Cloud Editor
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The official upload guide uses desktop Arduino IDE, while Cloud Editor adds an account and Cloud Agent route.

alternatives_considered.name: Arduino CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: The docs home and support first-upload guide select the graphical IDE route for board setup.

alternatives_considered.name: Arduino App Lab and UNO Q
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: App Lab is a board-family-specific application surface, not the general sketch-upload guide.

entry_point.developer_intent: Program a connected Arduino board for the first time with the current desktop Arduino IDE.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has a supported computer but Arduino IDE is not assumed installed.
The developer has an Arduino board and compatible data USB cable but no board package, board, or port selection is assumed.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Upload a sketch in Arduino IDE
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: Arduino IDE compiles and uploads a sketch to the selected board, and the sketch starts running on the hardware.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The upload finishes without an error and the selected sketch starts running on the board; when using the built-in Blink example named by the guide, the board's `LED_BUILTIN` behavior provides the visible program output on boards that expose it.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The official guide ends its upload section with `Your sketch will start running on the board. It will run again each time the board is reset.`

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: This is the first end-to-end proof across IDE installation, USB connection, board support package, board and port selection, compilation, transfer, and execution. Custom projects and cloud features follow later.

prerequisites.requirement: A supported Windows, macOS, or 64-bit Linux computer is required for Arduino IDE 2.
An Arduino board and a compatible data USB cable, not a charge-only cable, are required.
The computer must allow application installation and USB-device access; some operating systems or board packages can require driver or port permissions.
Network access is required to download Arduino IDE and any missing board package.

primary_path.action: Download the latest Arduino IDE 2 release for the computer's operating system and install or launch it using the official OS-specific instructions.
details: Windows runs the downloaded executable and follows the installer.
macOS opens the disk image and moves Arduino IDE to Applications; Linux marks the AppImage executable and launches it.
primary_path.input: Supported computer, installation permission, storage, and network access.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Unsupported OS, blocked installation, missing Linux FUSE dependency, or incomplete download prevents launch.

primary_path.action: Connect the Arduino board directly to the computer with a compatible data USB cable.
details: The USB connection powers the board and carries the program from the IDE.
Connector type varies by board family and computer.
primary_path.input: Arduino board, compatible data cable, and available USB port.
primary_path.output: Powered, physically connected board visible to the operating system or ready for detection.
primary_path.success_signal: The board powers on and appears as a port or unknown connected device in the IDE.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Charge-only cable, incompatible connector, hub problems, missing permission, damaged cable, or board state can prevent detection.

primary_path.action: Install the board package when Arduino IDE reports that support files for the detected board are missing.
details: In IDE 2, click Yes on the automatic package prompt.
If no prompt appears, continue; an unknown or unsupported selection may require manual Board Manager setup.
primary_path.input: Detected board identity and network access.
primary_path.output: Compiler, uploader, definitions, and related files for the board family.
primary_path.success_signal: The board can be selected without a missing-platform warning.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Unknown board identity, package-index or download failure, missing driver, or wrong package blocks compilation or upload.

primary_path.action: Select the correct board and serial port using the IDE 2 board selector.
details: Open the board selector and choose the port that shows the connected board.
If it appears as Unknown, use Select Other board and port; the Tools > Board and Tools > Port menus are the documented alternative.
primary_path.input: Connected board, installed package, and enumerated serial port.
primary_path.output: Arduino IDE configured for the exact board and upload port.
primary_path.success_signal: The board selector displays the intended board and port.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: No port, an unknown or incorrect board, busy port, Linux port permission, or missing driver requires the linked detection troubleshooting before proceeding.

primary_path.action: Open a sketch, using the guide's built-in Blink example through File > Examples > 01.Basics > Blink for a no-authoring first run.
details: The guide also permits writing a custom sketch.
Built-in examples are shipped in the IDE; most boards expose an onboard LED through `LED_BUILTIN`, but board behavior can vary.
primary_path.input: Configured Arduino IDE and selected board.
primary_path.output: Blink or another valid sketch open in the editor.
primary_path.success_signal: The intended sketch is loaded and ready to compile.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Board-specific example incompatibility or edited syntax errors can prevent compilation.

primary_path.action: Optionally click Verify to compile first, then click Upload and wait while the IDE compiles and programs the board.
details: Verify is optional in the official upload path because Upload performs compilation.
Do not disconnect or reset the board while the transfer is in progress.
primary_path.input: Valid sketch, correct board package, board selection, port, and working USB connection.
primary_path.output: Compiled program transferred to board flash memory.
primary_path.success_signal: The upload completes without a failed-upload error and the IDE no longer reports the transfer in progress.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Compile failure, wrong board or processor, unavailable or blocked port, lost USB connection, bootloader state, driver, or upload-tool error requires correction and another Upload attempt.

primary_path.action: Observe the uploaded sketch starting on the board.
details: The guide states that the sketch runs again on each board reset.
With the named Blink example and a board exposing `LED_BUILTIN`, observe the corresponding onboard LED program behavior.
primary_path.success_signal: The expected sketch behavior is visible or otherwise observable on the hardware; for Blink, the built-in LED changes state repeatedly on a compatible board.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A successful transfer with incorrect or absent behavior requires checking the sketch's expected output and board-specific LED or pin mapping; it is not yet verified first success.

branches.condition: The developer uses a Chromebook or chooses Arduino Cloud Editor.
branches.path: Leave the desktop-IDE route and use Arduino's Chromebook or Cloud Editor guide, including the Cloud Agent and account requirements where documented.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Installation, account, browser agent, detection, and upload interfaces change, though the board-programmed result can remain similar.

branches.condition: The IDE automatically identifies the board and all support files are already installed.
branches.path: Accept the recognized board in the board selector and skip package installation.

branches.condition: The board is unknown, absent, or incorrectly identified.
branches.path: Refresh after disconnect and reconnect, allow USB permissions, verify a direct data-cable connection and power, try another port or cable, use a supported bootloader reset where applicable, inspect the OS USB-device list, install board-specific drivers or firmware if the official troubleshooting identifies them, then return to the selector and choose the correct board and port.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Detection recovery adds hardware and operating-system steps before compilation can begin.

branches.path: Compile with Verify to isolate a code error; confirm board and port; reconnect with a working data cable; remove interfering shields or jumper wires; try a supported bootloader reset; close software holding the port; inspect the specific console error; apply board-specific guidance; then retry Upload.
branches.effect_on_first_success: The loop repeats at configuration or upload until a program transfers and runs.

friction_gates.description: The current desktop IDE is a separate OS-specific download and installation.
A compatible board, data-capable cable, connector, and usable computer port are required.
Each board family can require a separate board package and drivers before compilation and upload.
USB enumeration and serial-port access can require OS permission or Linux configuration.
The exact board model and serial port must match the connected hardware.
Board-package installation, compilation, and transfer must finish before execution can be checked.
A clean upload is followed by a hardware behavior check; the sketch starting on the board is the guide's terminal state.

sources.title: Upload a sketch in Arduino IDE
If your board is not detected by Arduino IDE
sections_used: Check for Unknown or incorrect board name

sources.title: If your sketch doesn't upload
sections_used: Select the correct board and port
Check for busy or blocked ports

uncertainties.question: What exact visible output does Blink produce on every Arduino-compatible board?
uncertainties.impact: LED color, active-high versus active-low behavior, or absence of an exposed onboard LED can change the final visual observation.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The platform-level upload guide promises that the sketch starts but does not enumerate every board's LED hardware; use the selected board's official guide if Blink behavior differs.

excluded_after_success: Editing or authoring a custom sketch
Sensors, actuators, shields, and external circuits
Arduino CLI, App Lab, MicroPython, and production device fleets
